title: Caramel popcorn
subtitle: Oven-dried caramel-coated popcorn with sea salt
excerpt: Popped corn coated in a butter and brown sugar caramel, then spread on trays and baked at low heat until the coating dries to a crisp, glossy shell. A pinch of bicarbonate of soda stirred into the hot caramel makes it foam slightly, producing a lighter, crisper result than plain caramel coating.
sourceNotes: Caramel popcorn is documented in American domestic cookbooks from the late nineteenth century, with 'Cracker Jack' (the commercial version) first sold in 1896. The home version using a butter and brown sugar caramel baked in the oven to set the coating is the established American domestic method, appearing widely in twentieth-century American domestic baking literature. The recipe here synthesises this formula.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles straightforwardly. Use two large baking trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for up to one week. Do not refrigerate — humidity softens the caramel coating.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 130°C. Low heat dries the caramel coating without burning it. Stir every 10 minutes to ensure even coating. The popcorn is done when the coating is dry, glossy, and hard to the touch after cooling on a piece.

glossaryTerms.definition: The crisp, glossy shell on caramel popcorn is formed in two stages: a hot caramel coating is poured over the popcorn, then the coated popcorn is baked at low heat to dry and set it. A small amount of bicarbonate of soda stirred into the hot caramel causes a brief foam, incorporating tiny air bubbles into the coating, which makes it lighter and crisper than a dense toffee shell. Work quickly when pouring the caramel — it begins to set within a minute.

content.text: is poured over the popcorn very hot and sets as the popcorn bakes. If hot caramel touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed. Work on a cleared surface with a large heatproof bowl and the popcorn ready before making the caramel.

Heat the oven to 150°C conventional. Line two large baking trays with baking paper. Pop the kernels: heat the oil in a large saucepan with a lid over high heat, add the kernels, cover, and shake every 30 seconds until the popping slows to more than 2 seconds between pops. Pour into a very large heatproof bowl, removing any unpopped kernels.

Melt the butter, brown sugar, golden syrup, salt, and vanilla in the saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a full boil without stirring and cook for 4 minutes — the caramel will deepen to a toffee colour. Remove from the heat. If hot caramel touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed. Stir in the bicarbonate of soda; it will foam. Pour immediately over the popcorn and stir quickly to coat evenly.

Spread the coated popcorn in a single layer across the lined trays. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes, stirring every 10 minutes, until the coating is dry and glossy all over. Spread on a clean sheet of baking paper to cool completely — the coating hardens as it cools. Break apart any clumps once cool and store in an airtight tin.
